title: 08_cited_clean_current_base
excerpt:
# Universities After AI: Epistemic Legitimation, Distributed Intellectual Production, and the Second-Tier University

Artificial intelligence first becomes visible to universities where inherited academic routines no longer certify what they used to certify. Assessment no longer reliably separates formed capability from assisted performance. Faculty work changes when explanation, feedback, drafting, and coding move into shared human–AI workflows. Research shifts when language models and agentic systems participate in framing questions, testing code, and comparing versions. A degree loses part of its evidentiary force when advanced intellectual outputs can be produced without the capabilities those outputs were supposed to prove.

The first institutional responses usually appear at the administrative and compliance layer. Universities issue rules for AI use, redesign assessment, and train faculty for new workflows. These measures reduce immediate disorder, but they do not touch the deeper shift: the older link between academic output, formed capability, assessment, and degree certification no longer holds by itself. Platform and vendor infrastructures are beginning to organize the formats in which questions are asked, evidence is checked, authorship is attributed, and competence is recognized. They set default environments for producing answers and, through those environments, reshape the epistemic and procedural order around knowledge production. If the university responds only through compliance, workflow adaptation, or tool regulation, it allows external infrastructures to define the conditions under which intellectual work becomes credible (Bommasani et al. 2021; Bender et al. 2021; Suchman 2007).

The university becomes institutionally necessary when a fragile, local, contested, or emerging practice has to acquire public form: it must become teachable, disputable, assessable, and inheritable. This function can be called epistemic legitimation. The university tur
